decisions.decision: Agent input schema validation must be enforced at dispatch time, not decorative
decisions.rationale: Analysis of pi-subagents vs pi-workflows revealed that our agent input schemas are parsed into AgentSpec.inputSchema but never validated before dispatch. 16 of 24 agents skip input schemas entirely, and workflows like resumable-analysis pass incomplete data to synthesizer (2 of 3 required fields) without error. Unenforced schemas are worse than no schemas — they create false confidence in typed composition. Either validate or remove.

decisions.decision: Template composition (extends/blocks/macros) must be demonstrated across more agent families, not just analyzers
decisions.rationale: Only 1 of 22 template directories uses Nunjucks inheritance. The analyzer family (structure/quality/patterns extending base-analyzer) proves the model works, but 21 other template families are flat — no composition. If the machinery isn't exercised, it's over-engineering. Refactoring existing agents to use shared templates validates the architecture and reduces prompt duplication.

decisions.decision: Add ad-hoc single-agent invocation outside of workflow specs
decisions.rationale: pi-subagents proves users want to compose agents without writing YAML specs. Every use of our system requires a .workflow.yaml file. A /workflow run-agent <name> <task> command (or equivalent tool) that dispatches a single agent step with typed input from the command line bridges this gap without abandoning our spec-driven model.

decisions.decision: Adopt worktree isolation for parallel steps from pi-subagents' design
decisions.rationale: Our parallel: step type runs agents concurrently in the same working tree. Any workflow with parallel agent steps that write files risks filesystem conflicts. pi-subagents' worktree.ts (326 lines) is a production-grade solution: temp git worktrees per agent, node_modules symlink, diff capture, cleanup. This is a latent bug in our parallel execution.

decisions.decision: Context forking (session branching for subprocesses) is desirable but deferred until pi exposes the API
decisions.rationale: pi-subagents' fork-context.ts creates real branched sessions from the parent's current leaf. Our agent steps always start fresh — zero context from the parent conversation. This would improve quality for context-dependent steps but requires pi's sessionManager.createBranchedSession() API, which is not available in our subprocess dispatch model (we spawn pi --mode json, not a library call).

decisions.decision: Async/background workflow execution is valuable but deferred to phase 3
decisions.rationale: pi-subagents has mature async support (detached runners, durable status files, TUI widgets, session-scoped notifications). Our workflows are strictly foreground. For long-running workflows this blocks the conversation. However, our checkpoint/resume already provides the key benefit (don't lose work on interruption). Async is UX polish, not correctness.

decisions.decision: Recursion depth guard required before implementing Phase 6 nested workflow invocation
decisions.rationale: pi-subagents' PI_SUBAGENT_MAX_DEPTH env-var pattern is the right approach for subprocess-based architectures. Must be in place before the workflow: step type is unblocked.

decisions.decision: Our YAML agent specs with Nunjucks templates afford superior recombinability over pi-subagents' markdown-with-frontmatter format, but only when the composition primitives are actually used
decisions.rationale: Comparative analysis proved: (1) Template inheritance (extends/blocks) enables a single base template to produce specialized agents without forking — pi-subagents cannot do this. (2) Parametric task templates ({{ input.field }}) adapt prompts to typed input — pi-subagents uses only {task}/{previous}/{chain_dir} string interpolation. (3) Schema-validated output ensures downstream consumers get typed data — pi-subagents returns raw text. However, in practice only 8/24 agents define input schemas, only 1/22 template families use inheritance, and reused agents receive identical input every time. The advantage is architectural, not realized.

decisions.decision: Schema-level if/then enforcement for task completion gate, not SDK-level or tool-level
decisions.rationale: The v0.11.0 completeTask() SDK function gated task completion on verification but left updateItemInBlock, update-block-item tool, and block workflow step type ungated. Adding a parallel gated path next to an unrestricted original is not enforcement. JSON Schema draft-07 if/then on the tasks item schema — if status=completed then verification required — enforces at the AJV validation layer inside writeBlock(), which all write paths funnel through. No code changes to block-api needed. The schema is the universal gate.

decisions.decision: Completed-without-verification is error severity in validateProject, not warning
decisions.rationale: Schema enforcement prevents corruption through the API. validateProject catches corruption from outside the API (manual file edits, legacy data). A completed task without verification is a structural integrity violation, not a data quality issue. Error severity means validateProject returns valid: false, surfacing the problem as a hard failure rather than an advisory.

decisions.decision: Format-json output parse failure is fatal — aligns agent step output with block write validation contract
decisions.rationale: The format-json path (step-agent.ts:232-238) silently swallowed JSON.parse failures, allowing steps declaring output.format: json to complete successfully with output: undefined. This violated the principle established in dec-009: an ungated path parallel to a gated one is not enforcement. The schema-bound path already failed on bad JSON (line 220-221). The format-json path must follow the same contract. Block writes are fatal on validation failure (block-api writeBlock); agent output validation must be equally strict. Silent swallow created untraceable cascading failures in downstream expression resolution.
